meta.title: Flashcard Content Supplement
meta.description: Atomic flashcard sets (12–14 per module) across all nine curriculum modules. Includes Leitner Box promotion logic in the implementation_notes section.

leitner_system.description: Basic/Advanced cards function as a two-stage learning unit.
leitner_system.advanced_introduction_trigger: Basic card answered correctly twice in a row while in Box 4 (once to promote to Box 4, once more on its 7-day review).
leitner_system.advanced_failure_reinstatement: If the Advanced card is answered incorrectly twice consecutively, the paired Basic card is reinstated to Box 2.

modules.title: Why Skills Matter: The Problem With Prompts

cards.front: What is a brittle prompt?
cards.back: An ad-hoc conversational prompt that lacks a deterministic behavioral contract.

cards.front: What is a skill.markdown file?
cards.back: A structured markdown file providing a deterministic behavioral contract for an LLM to execute.

cards.front: Where must a skill.markdown file be stored?
cards.back: In a dedicated, version-controlled directory.

cards.front: What four components must a skill primitive define?
cards.back: A clear name, defined inputs, specified outputs, and documented edge cases.

cards.front: What is the primary benefit of storing skills in version control?
cards.back: It makes them auditable, testable, updatable, and recoverable.

cards.front: What is the 'skill as a ZIP file' analogy?
cards.back: A skill condenses complex behavioral instructions into a single reliably-routable primitive.

cards.front: How does a skill primitive affect an LLM's cognitive load?
cards.back: It reduces working memory load in the same way chunking does for human memory.

cards.front: Why is a skill.markdown written in markdown?
cards.back: So that an LLM can natively read and execute the behavioral contract.

cards.front: Why is a conversational prompt considered brittle even if it currently produces the right output?
cards.back: It lacks a guaranteed output schema, meaning subtle vendor model updates can silently alter its format.

cards.front: How does a skill primitive prevent prompt decay when team members change?
cards.back: It replaces individual conversational phrasing with a single versioned trigger and deterministic schema.

cards.front: What is the structural analogy between a skill.markdown file and a software function?
cards.back: Both have a clearly defined scope, explicit input constraints, and strict output contracts.

cards.front: Where does the software function analogy for skills break down?
cards.back: LLM execution introduces stochastic variance, requiring explicit Edge Cases that compiled code handles mechanically.

cards.front: What are the two structural parts of a skill.markdown file?
cards.back: A frontmatter metadata section and an operational body section.

cards.front: What is the Routing Description?
cards.back: The single-line frontmatter field an LLM gateway uses to match user intent to a skill.

cards.front: What two elements must a Routing Description contain?
cards.back: Concrete trigger phrases and the specified output shape.

cards.front: Why must the description field be a single continuous line?
cards.back: LLM routing parsers process the description as a single-line token.

cards.front: What is the 150-Line Constraint?
cards.back: The maximum recommended length for a skill.markdown file.

cards.front: What are the five subsystems of a skill body?
cards.back: Reasoning Frameworks, Deterministic Output Specifications, Edge Cases, Exemplars, and Contextual Economy.

cards.front: What is the purpose of the Reasoning Framework subsystem?
cards.back: To encode declarative decision principles that allow the model to generalize to unanticipated inputs.

cards.front: What does a Deterministic Output Specification declare?
cards.back: The exact schema of the skill output, including column names, row counts, and field lengths.

cards.front: What is Edge Case Documentation?
cards.back: Explicit instructions that replace the model's default hallucination behavior with defined policy.

cards.front: What is a Pattern-Matching Exemplar?
cards.back: Static example output stored in the skill directory to provide few-shot performance anchoring.

cards.front: What failure occurs if a text editor auto-wraps a routing description across multiple lines?
cards.back: Routing failure. The parser reads only the first line, rendering the remaining routing criteria invisible.

cards.front: Why does the 150-Line Constraint exist in production environments?
cards.back: Enterprise gateways inject surrounding system context at runtime; oversized skills cause context window overrun.

cards.front: Why are step-by-step procedural instructions a reliability risk in a skill body?
cards.back: They are brittle. The model has no decision policy to handle inputs that fall between the enumerated steps.

cards.front: A skill requires a 60-line exemplar, pushing the file to 170 lines. What is the structural fix?
cards.back: Store the exemplar as a separate text file in the skill directory to maintain the 150-line Contextual Economy constraint.

cards.front: What is the standard formula for a Routing Description?
cards.back: "Triggers on [TRIGGER PHRASE] to [ACTION VERB] [OUTPUT ARTIFACT]: [SCHEMA DETAILS]."

cards.front: What is the very first step when building a skill from scratch?
cards.back: Define the trigger condition and exact output shape in plain English.

cards.front: What is 'Mistake 1: The Vague Trigger'?
cards.back: Using semantically diffuse phrases like "helps users" that fail to provide a routing anchor.

cards.front: What is 'Mistake 2: The Procedural Body'?
cards.back: Writing sequential "Step 1, Step 2" instructions instead of declarative rules.

cards.front: What is 'Mistake 3: The Ambiguous Output'?
cards.back: Requesting output with vague verbs like "write up" instead of specifying an exact schema.

cards.front: Why do downstream agents require a Deterministic Output Specification?
cards.back: Because agents parse output mechanically and cannot tolerate structural ambiguity.

cards.front: What happens when a model encounters a situation without a documented Edge Case?
cards.back: It defaults to hallucinating plausible-sounding, but uncontrolled, output.

cards.front: Should formatting constraints like "No markdown headers" be included in the Output Specification?
cards.back: Yes. Negative constraints are critical for maintaining strict schema adherence.

cards.front: A description reads: 'Assists with competitive analysis.' Name its two critical flaws.
cards.back: It lacks concrete trigger phrases and fails to specify an output artifact/shape.

cards.front: How does specifying output shape directly in the Routing Description improve skill performance?
cards.back: It provides the model with a clear goal state immediately upon triggering, reducing format drift.

cards.front: An author writes 'Step 1: Read text. Step 2: Extract tasks.' How does a Reasoning Framework fix this flaw?
cards.back: It replaces brittle sequence with declarative logic (e.g., 'Action = explicitly stated task'), allowing generalization to novel inputs.

cards.front: A skill must process input transcripts of variable length up to 10,000 words. How should this be handled?
cards.back: Explicitly define a truncation policy within the Edge Cases subsystem (e.g., 'Process first 5,000 words only').

cards.front: What specific consumer are Level 2 skills designed for?
cards.back: Autonomous agents executing pipelines.

cards.front: What fundamentally shifts when designing for agents rather than humans?
cards.back: The skill becomes a strict API specification rather than a flexible set of instructions.

cards.front: What are the four structural blocks of an Agentic SLA Contract?
cards.back: Input Contract, Output Contract, Dependency Contract, and Downstream Assumption Contract.

cards.front: What does an Input Contract declare?
cards.back: The exact input fields, their data types, formats, and whether they are required or optional.

cards.front: What does an Output Contract declare?
cards.back: The guaranteed success schema and all defined machine-parseable failure states.

cards.front: How must failure states be emitted in an agentic skill?
cards.back: As machine-parseable STATUS_CODEs.

cards.front: What does a Dependency Contract declare?
cards.back: Upstream preconditions like formatting or encoding that must hold for the skill to succeed.

cards.front: What does a Downstream Assumption Contract declare?
cards.back: Guarantees the calling agent can rely on, such as absolute sort orders or strict date formats.

cards.front: Why must agentic skills return STATUS_CODEs instead of natural language error messages?
cards.back: Automated pipelines crash when their mechanical parsing logic encounters conversational failure prose.

cards.front: Explain the 'recipe vs. API' analogy regarding agentic skill output.
cards.back: A recipe relies on human judgment ('add to taste'); an API requires strict, mechanical parameter specifications.

cards.front: A skill outputs a 'Date' field as a plain text string. What specific change makes this agent-ready?
cards.back: The Output Contract must specify a machine-parseable encoding format, such as ISO 8601.

cards.front: What is the operational consequence if a Dependency Contract (e.g., UTF-8 encoding) is violated?
cards.back: The skill fails predictably via a STATUS_CODE rather than producing silent, corrupted output downstream.

cards.front: What is composability in a skill pipeline?
cards.back: When an upstream skill's output natively serves as a downstream skill's input without human intervention.

cards.front: What is a silent pipeline failure?
cards.back: When a pipeline continues executing with subtly corrupted data without crashing or emitting an error.

cards.front: Why are silent composability failures particularly dangerous?
cards.back: They produce plausible but incorrect results that may only be caught much later by downstream consumers.

cards.front: What is the core design rule for building composable pipelines?
cards.back: Design Output Contracts first, then work backwards to design the Reasoning Framework.

cards.front: What is the Placeholder Field strategy?
cards.back: Adding a default-value field to upstream output to maintain strict schema consistency for downstream consumers.

cards.front: What is the Deterministic Script Rule?
cards.back: Pipeline steps requiring absolute, zero-variance execution must be hardwired scripts, not LLM skills.

cards.front: What makes LLMs inappropriate for zero-tolerance deterministic tasks?
cards.back: Their inherent stochastic variance.

cards.front: What is the purpose of a Data Flow Diagram in pipeline design?
cards.back: To explicitly document the schema handoff points between skills.

cards.front: Skill A outputs 3 fields. Skill B expects 4. How does the Placeholder Field strategy resolve this?
cards.back: Skill A adds a 4th field with a null/0 value, ensuring the structural schema perfectly matches Skill B's Input Contract.

cards.front: Why does 'output-first design' prevent silent composability failures?
cards.back: It forces the upstream skill's generation logic to perfectly conform to the exact structural requirements of the downstream consumer.

cards.front: A pipeline requires inserting validated records into a production database. Should this be an LLM skill? Justify.
cards.back: No. Inserting records has zero error tolerance, triggering the Deterministic Script Rule which mandates a hardwired script.

cards.front: A pipeline requires parsing a fixed-column legacy CSV. Why is this better suited for a Python script than a skill?
cards.back: It requires exact deterministic execution without reasoning latitude, which falls under the Deterministic Script Rule.

cards.front: What are the three primary categories of skill failures?
cards.back: Routing Failure, Output Drift, and Pipeline Break.

cards.front: What defines a Routing Failure?
cards.back: The skill never invokes despite a matching user query.

cards.front: What defines Output Drift?
cards.back: The skill invokes correctly, but its output structure varies unpredictably between runs.

cards.front: What defines a Pipeline Break?
cards.back: An upstream skill succeeds, but the downstream skill fails silently or emits an INVALID_INPUT status.

cards.front: What is the most common cause of a Routing Failure?
cards.back: A vague or multi-line routing description.

cards.front: What is the most common cause of Output Drift?
cards.back: An insufficiently deterministic Output Specification that leaves room for model interpretation.

cards.front: What is the most common cause of a Pipeline Break?
cards.back: A schema mismatch between an upstream Output Contract and a downstream Input Contract.

cards.front: What is the final step on the debugging checklist if all syntax is correct?
cards.back: Verify the file is under 150 lines and split the skill if necessary.

cards.front: A skill description contains line breaks in the raw file. What precise mechanical failure does this cause?
cards.back: Routing Failure. The parser treats the description as a single token and ignores all criteria after the first line break.

cards.front: An agent intermittently fails to parse a skill's output. What is the first diagnostic step?
cards.back: Diagnose as Output Drift; check the Output Specification for prose-style instructions and replace them with absolute schemas.

cards.front: Skill A and Skill B work perfectly in isolation. Chained together, Skill B emits INVALID_INPUT. What must you check?
cards.back: Diagnose as a Pipeline Break; conduct a field-by-field schema check including names, delimiters, and encoding formats.

cards.front: A developer replaces a sequential 10-step instruction list with a declarative decision policy. What failure mode does this prevent?
cards.back: Output Drift caused by unhandled edge cases breaking the brittle procedural sequence.

cards.front: What are the three tiers in the skills governance hierarchy?
cards.back: Tier 1 (Org Standards), Tier 2 (Team Methodology), and Tier 3 (Personal Workflow).

cards.front: Who owns Tier 1 skills?
cards.back: Enterprise Administrators.

cards.front: Who must author or review Tier 2 skills?
cards.back: Business-Unit Teams and Senior Practitioners.

cards.front: Who owns Tier 3 skills?
cards.back: Individual Contributors.

cards.front: What type of knowledge is encoded in Tier 1 skills?
cards.back: Universally applicable standards like brand voice or formatting schemas.

cards.front: What type of knowledge is encoded in Tier 2 skills?
cards.back: High-value institutional methodology specific to a domain.

cards.front: What is the primary continuity risk associated with Tier 3 skills?
cards.back: Storing them locally, meaning the skill is lost when the employee departs.

cards.front: What is tier promotion?
cards.back: The process of generalizing a high-value Tier 3 skill and elevating it to a Tier 2 team repository.

cards.front: Why is it an operational risk for an IT administrator to author a Tier 2 M&A skill?
cards.back: They lack the domain expertise; the skill may be syntactically valid but operationally incorrect.

cards.front: How does tier promotion resolve personal continuity risk?
cards.back: By stripping personal idiosyncrasies and committing the skill to a version-controlled team repository.

cards.front: Contrast the update cadence between Tier 1 and Tier 3 skills.
cards.back: Tier 1 updates are infrequent and require formal change control; Tier 3 updates are rapid and highly iterative.

cards.front: A team member keeps five custom automation skills on their laptop desktop. What specific governance failure is this?
cards.back: Tier-3 Loss vulnerability. Individual contributor skills must be committed to a team monorepo to prevent a single point of failure.

cards.front: What is the Transformer Sensitivity Problem?
cards.back: Small vocabulary changes in one skill shifting semantic probability distributions across other skills in the same context.

cards.front: What are the three components of a quantitative testing harness?
cards.back: Static Test Cases, a Numeric Scoring Rubric, and a Version Comparison Protocol.

cards.front: What defines a Static Test Case?
cards.back: A fixed, unchanging input-output pair used as a baseline for measuring all versions of a skill.

cards.front: What are the three required categories for static test cases?
cards.back: Happy path, edge case, and failure path.

cards.front: What scale is used in the numeric scoring rubric?
cards.back: A 1 to 5 scale based on verifiable structural criteria.

cards.front: What defines an objective rubric criterion?
cards.back: It can be mathematically verified by a parser (e.g., zero parsing errors).

cards.front: What is the maximum acceptable score drop for any single test case during an update?
cards.back: 1 point.

cards.front: What output state earns a score of 1 on the numeric rubric?
cards.back: A prose explanation, apology, or hallucination containing no structured data.

cards.front: A developer changes 'concise' to 'brief' in a Tier 1 skill, causing a Tier 2 skill to fail. What is the mechanism?
cards.back: Transformer sensitivity. The vocabulary tweak shifted context-wide probabilities, triggering a cross-skill regression.

cards.front: Why must regression testing evaluate adjacent skills rather than just the updated skill?
cards.back: Because manual testing only catches isolated syntax errors, missing the systemic probability shifts that impact shared context.

cards.front: A rubric reads: 'Score 5 if the tone is highly professional.' Why does this invalidate the test harness?
cards.back: It relies on subjective evaluator bias, preventing consistent, automated apples-to-apples version comparisons.

cards.front: If a proposed skill update causes an adjacent skill's test case to drop by 3 points, what is the protocol response?
cards.back: Halt deployment. The cross-skill regression exceeds the threshold and must be investigated or compensated for.

cards.front: What is the Version Control Imperative?
cards.back: The non-negotiable requirement that all skills at every tier be committed to a version-controlled repository.

cards.front: Why are skills treated as organizational intellectual property?
cards.back: They encode operational logic and institutional methodology equivalent to source code.

cards.front: What are the three enterprise continuity risks for deployed skills?
cards.back: Skill Orphaning, Silent Regression, and Tier-3 Loss.

cards.front: What triggers Skill Orphaning?
cards.back: The departure of the only senior practitioner who understands the methodology of a Tier 2 skill.

cards.front: What triggers a Silent Regression in enterprise deployment?
cards.back: A vendor model update that degrades skill output quality without triggering an explicit error.

cards.front: What triggers Tier-3 Loss?
cards.back: An employee leaving the organization with high-value workflow skills stored only on personal hardware.

cards.front: What is the primary mitigation for Skill Orphaning?
cards.back: Quarterly documentation reviews and requiring explanatory README files detailing the business context.

cards.front: What is the primary mitigation for Silent Regressions?
cards.back: Running automated quantitative regression suites against the new model before deploying the update.

cards.front: Why does an org-skills monorepo explicitly require a tier-3 directory?
cards.back: To provide a designated location for individual contributor skills, removing the pressure for risky local storage.

cards.front: An LLM model update causes 2 of 6 test cases for a Tier 2 skill to drop to a score of 2. What is the operational response?
cards.back: Do not deploy the model update for this gateway. Invoke the rollback playbook and investigate the regressions.

cards.front: Why is 'best practice' an inadequate descriptor for version-controlling skill infrastructure?
cards.back: Without version control, skills completely lack auditability, recoverability, and transferability — making it a mandatory structural requirement.

cards.front: How does an offboarding checklist mitigate Tier-3 continuity risk?
cards.back: By enforcing the policy that all locally developed automations must be pushed to the team monorepo before an employee's access is revoked.
